claim: YOLO demand evidence system properly distinguishes project, external, user, and unknown evidence scopes across schema, prompt, readiness contract, tests, and documentation. Project facts require project-scoped evidence with repo-relative paths; external evidence cannot prove target-project state.

evidence.summary: Evidence schema definition requires scope field and explains project evidence requirements.
evidence.why: This is the core schema contract that all evidence agents must follow.

evidence.summary: VALID_EVIDENCE_SCOPES set restricts scope to four valid values: project, external, user, unknown.
evidence.why: This enforces that only these four scope values are acceptable in evidence records.

evidence.summary: evidenceRecordScope() function auto-detects scope from path, url, and source fields with detailed inference rules.
evidence.why: This provides the scope inference logic when agents don't explicitly declare scope.

evidence.summary: evidenceRecordHasDeclaredScope() validates that evidence records explicitly declare valid scope.
evidence.why: This is used during evidence normalization to catch missing scope declarations.

evidence.summary: projectEvidenceBlockingIssues() enforces that project facts require project-scoped evidence with concrete path locators.
evidence.why: This blocks external-only evidence from being used for existing-project factual claims.

evidence.summary: PRD readiness inspection checks for missing scope declarations and project evidence path requirements.
evidence.why: This is the final gate that prevents PRD readiness when evidence scope contract is violated.

evidence.summary: Test case: external-only evidence cannot satisfy existing project facts.
evidence.why: This validates that the readiness contract rejects external evidence for project claims.

evidence.summary: Test case: project evidence requires a concrete project locator (path).
evidence.why: This ensures that project evidence must include repo-relative file paths.

evidence.summary: Test case: external URLs cannot be relabeled as project evidence.
evidence.why: This prevents agents from circumventing scope rules by mixing URL and scope fields.

evidence.summary: Test case: evidence records must declare valid scope explicitly.
evidence.why: This validates that missing scope fields are caught as readiness blockers.

evidence.summary: Agent prompt explicitly instructs providers to declare scope on every evidence record and explains project evidence requirements.
evidence.why: This is the direct instruction to evidence agents about scope discipline.

evidence.summary: Evidence normalization checks scope declarations and blocks agents that omit scope field.
evidence.why: This enforces scope validation immediately after agent output is parsed.

evidence.summary: Test case: agent evidence without explicit scope is blocked during normalization.
evidence.why: This validates that dispatch runtime catches missing scope declarations.

evidence.summary: Doctrine document explains evidence scope requirements: project evidence needs repo-relative paths; external evidence is background-only.
evidence.why: This provides the human-readable explanation of why scope distinctions matter.

evidence.summary: Stress test validates evidence schema and prompt support research/fetch evidence without mixing external and project facts.
evidence.why: This demonstrates real-world validation of evidence scope separation under agent stress.

evidence.summary: Stress test artifacts show repeated validation of evidence scope contract across multiple dispatch runs.
evidence.why: These artifacts provide empirical evidence that the scope contract works under real Claude agent execution.

risks: Evidence agents could still ignore scope instructions and return malformed records; but normalization and readiness checks will block these.
External research tools might be unavailable during agent execution; but agents are instructed to put this in missing rather than treating it as project fact.

result.notes: Evidence scope system is comprehensively implemented across schema, validation, tests, prompts, and documentation. All four layers (schema, readiness contract, dispatch prompt, and tests) enforce that project facts require project-scoped evidence with concrete paths, and external evidence cannot prove target-project state. The acceptance criteria are fully satisfied.
